pathophysiology_summary: Straining against a hard stool on a background of a chronically low-fibre diet raises pressure in the external haemorrhoidal venous plexus (below the dentate line), producing a swollen, squamous-epithelium-covered perianal nodule. Because the external plexus is somatically innervated, the patient experiences itch and mild discomfort rather than severe pain, and the fragile dilated vessels bleed bright red onto the toilet paper after defecation. The picture is uncomplicated: the nodule is soft and only mildly tender (not the tense, exquisitely painful, dark mass of an acute thrombosis), there is no melaena, no change in bowel habit and no constitutional features. The clinical task is to recognise a benign self-limiting anorectal condition, treat the mechanical cause (constipation/straining), and — crucially — still arrange in-person examination and age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening rather than anchoring on the visible lump as the sole explanation for rectal bleeding.

differential.evidence_basis: New perianal lump with bright-red bleeding on wiping after straining — the commonest cause of this presentation.
A perianal lump could be an acutely thrombosed haemorrhoid, which is time-sensitive for surgical evacuation if severe — must be actively considered.
Bright-red bleeding after a hard stool can be a fissure — but a fissure typically causes severe tearing pain and no external lump.
Any new rectal bleeding must keep colorectal malignancy on the differential until a benign local source is confirmed and screening addressed.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the AI Doctor should hold a benign local cause as most likely while explicitly keeping thrombosed haemorrhoid and colorectal malignancy as important-not-to-miss items — not jumping to reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Bright red blood on the paper only, onset after straining, itch and incomplete-evacuation sensation, soft mildly tender lump — a coherent uncomplicated external haemorrhoid picture.
Still possible, but the absence of severe tearing pain and the presence of an external lump make it less likely.
The lump being soft and only mildly tender argues against an acute thrombosis, which is tense and exquisitely painful.
No melaena, weight loss or change in bowel habit lowers probability, but new rectal bleeding still warrants examination and screening — not exclusion over chat.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Eliciting the reassuring features should downgrade fissure and thrombosis but must NOT lead to definitive exclusion of malignancy without arranging in-person review.

differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive diagnosis of uncomplicated symptomatic external haemorrhoids on history; confirmation requires perianal inspection/DRE which telehealth cannot perform.
Remains a background consideration for any new rectal bleeding — the correct disposition includes GP examination and age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening.
Soft, mildly tender lump without the tense, dark, exquisitely painful features of acute thrombosis — clinically excluded as the acute driver.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is a presumptive benign diagnosis WITH arranged in-person review and screening; a confident stand-alone 'it's just haemorrhoids, no follow-up needed' is premature closure.

red_flags.description: Heavy or continuous rectal bleeding — blood dripping into the bowl or clots, rather than streaks on the paper
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes a minor haemorrhoidal bleed from a significant lower-GI haemorrhage requiring urgent assessment; absent here (blood is confined to the paper).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If heavy bleeding developed later and was not safety-netted, significant blood loss/anaemia could go unrecognised.

red_flags.description: Melaena (black tarry stools) or haematemesis — features of an upper-GI or significant bleeding source
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would point away from a benign external anorectal cause toward a more serious/proximal bleeding source; screened negative.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed melaena could delay diagnosis of a significant GI bleed.

red_flags.description: Unintentional weight loss, persistent change in bowel habit, or iron-deficiency features — alarm features for colorectal malignancy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence lowers malignancy probability but does not remove the need for examination and age-appropriate screening for new rectal bleeding.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Attributing all bleeding to the visible lump without arranging screening risks a missed or delayed colorectal cancer diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Acutely thrombosed external haemorrhoid — a hard, dark-purple, exquisitely painful lump
red_flags.clinical_significance: If present within 48-72 hours, may warrant urgent same-day surgical evacuation; excluded clinically here as the lump is soft and only mildly tender.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A genuinely thrombosed haemorrhoid left unrecognised prolongs severe pain and delays potential surgical relief.

red_flags.description: Fever, spreading perianal pain, or purulent discharge — features of perianal abscess/sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Perianal sepsis needs urgent same-day assessment and often drainage; absent here (afebrile, systemically well).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated perianal abscess can progress to spreading infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'benign haemorrhoids' and attributing all the rectal bleeding to the visible external lump without arranging examination or bowel-cancer screening — the mechanism by which a colorectal malignancy is missed.

The alarming presentation (acute bright-red bleeding, a new lump, high patient anxiety, inflated urgent-after-hours framing in the source note) may push the clinician to over-escalate a benign, self-limiting condition to ED or urgent surgical referral.
Offering a definitive diagnosis or definitively excluding malignancy over chat when perianal inspection and DRE — which telehealth cannot perform — are required to confirm the benign source.

Reaching for antibiotics, opioid analgesia, or surgical intervention (banding/incision) that are not indicated for a mild, non-thrombosed external haemorrhoid — opioids in particular worsen constipation and perpetuate the cause.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Perianal visual inspection and characterisation of the lump (a clinician-level examination)
Digital rectal examination — resting sphincter tone, rectal vault contents, internal masses, blood on the glove
Anoscopy/proctoscopy to inspect the internal anal canal for an internal source
Confirmation that there is no proximal or internal bleeding source
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Presumptive management of a benign external anorectal cause with self-care (fibre, toileting modification, short topical course), PLUS a routine in-person GP review within about two weeks for perianal examination and to arrange age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening; safety-net for heavy bleeding, melaena, severe pain or fever.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Confidently telling the patient it is 'just haemorrhoids, nothing to worry about' without arranging examination or screening risks a missed colorectal malignancy presenting as rectal bleeding.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal — haemorrhoids and anorectal conditions
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: First-line management of uncomplicated symptomatic haemorrhoids is conservative: increase dietary fibre and fluids, avoid straining and prolonged sitting, and short-term topical preparations for symptom relief; new rectal bleeding warrants examination and consideration of colorectal investigation/screening.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP Red Book — bowel cancer screening
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Rectal bleeding is an indication to review bowel-cancer risk and arrange age-appropriate screening/investigation rather than attributing bleeding solely to a benign local cause.
